Food Chain Magnate is a heavy strategy game about building a fast food chain. The focus is on building your company using a 
card-driven (human) resource management system. Players compete on a variable city map through purchasing, marketing, 
and sales, and on a job market for key staff members. The game can be played by 2-5 serious gamers in 2-4 hours.

Introductory Game
Food Chain Magnate is a very unforgiving game. If you make a mistake, 
it can be very hard or even impossible to get back on track. For your first 
game, we strongly recommend you modify the rules as follows:
Do not use bank reserve cards.
Do not use milestones.
Ensure that all 3 drink supplier 
types are on the map.
Start with $75 per player in 
the bank (instead of $50).
Do not pay any salaries (skip the 
Payday phase each round).
End the game the first 
time the bank breaks.
This will make for a somewhat shorter 
game where everyone can get to 
know the rules without running their 
companies into the ground. For 
your second game, we recommend 
making these same modifications, 
but adding in milestones. 
NOTE: Some milestones do not provide 
useful benefits in an introductory game.

In Food Chain Magnate, play as a lone entrepreneur attempting to build your company into a thriving restaurant empire. You do this by 
hiring and training employees, running marketing campaigns to generate demand, and serving food and drink to paying customers.
Object of the Game
Your goal is to have the most total cash at the end of the game. The game ends after the bank breaks (i.e., runs out of cash) 
twice. The first time the bank breaks, players’ chosen bank reserve cards are revealed, and the bank is refilled with the 
total amount of cash shown on them. The second time the bank breaks, players finish collecting their earnings for 
the round, and then the game ends, and whoever has the most cash wins (pg. 28 and pg. 33 for details).  
The amount of cash each player has is always public information.
Choosing Bank Reserve Cards
The bank reserve cards you and your 
opponents choose at setup determine 
how long the game will last; and also, 
how many CEO slots each player will 
have after the first bank break!  
Playing a high-value card increases 
the total amount of cash that can be 
earned before triggering the end of the 
game, while playing a low-value card 
reduces it.
Longer or shorter games favor different 
strategies, but be aware that you will not 
initially know whether your opponents 
chose to shorten or lengthen the 
game, so flexibility can be important.
NOTE: First-time players will likely 
not be familiar with long- or short-term 
strategies, so we recommend playing 
without bank reserve cards in an 
introductory game, (pg. 5).
Game Concepts
Employee Cards
Employee cards represent the people who work for your restaurant. You start the game with only a CEO, but you can Hire cooks, 
waitresses, marketeers, managers, and others to perform various restaurant tasks. 
At the start of each round, each player simultaneously chooses employees from their hand to play into Work slots, then your 
remaining un-played employees go “On the beach” (in a stack above your CEO card, pg. 12). Your played employees determine 
the actions available to you during the round.

Employee Types (continues on next page)
Managers provide slots that expand your company 
structure and allow you to play more employees 
each round (pg. 12). They can also be trained into 
a variety of other employee types (pgs. 7-8).
Waitresses and CFOs provide extra cash at the end of 
Dinnertime (pg. 27). New business developers place 
new customer houses and gardens on the map (pg. 24).
Local and Regional Managers allow you to place new 
restaurants or move existing ones on the map (pg. 25). 
They also add drive-ins to your restaurants, which 
temporarily give them entrances at all 4 corners (pg. 17).
Pricing employees temporarily lower or raise the 
item price you charge customers. Lowering your 
price helps you compete for more customers; raising 
it makes you more money off each sale (pg. 27).
Recruiters hire new entry-level employees (pg. 15). 
Trainers train the employees you’ve hired into 
higher-level types (pg. 15).

Item Counters
Food counters (burgers and pizzas) and drink counters (soda, lemonade, and beer) are collectively referred to as item counters. 
These are used to track a variety of things during the game. The number of item counters in the game is not intended to be 
limited; if you run out of any item, use a suitable proxy. 
NOTE: Demand and duration are both tracked by item counters.
Stock items in front of you represent 
the food and drinks your restaurants 
have available to sell at Dinnertime 
(pg. 26).

Demand counters on a house show 
what the customers who live there 
want to buy. A house can have up to 
3 demand counters on it at a time, 
or 5 if it has a garden (pg. 24).
Duration counters on a marketing 
campaign show what item it is creating 
demand for and how many more 
rounds it has left to run (pg. 30).
Milestones
Milestones are rewards you receive 
for achieving certain goals during the 
game. Each milestone provides an 
immediate or ongoing benefit that 
can change the course of the game. 
It is vital to plan your strategy around 
which milestones you can claim!
When you fulfill the requirements for an 
available milestone, place a checkmark 
token covering it on your milestone tray 
and announce that you have claimed 
it. If you fulfill an available milestone’s 
requirements in the same round as one 
or more of your opponents, each of you 
claims the milestone and receives its 
full benefits. At the end of the round 
in which a milestone is claimed, each 
player who did not claim the milestone 
must place an 
 token covering it 
to indicate it is no longer available.
All benefits of each milestone are 
mandatory, meaning you must apply 
them even when they are not helpful to 
you. For example, if you have the “First 
Billboard Campaign” milestone, then all 
future marketing campaigns you launch 
must be eternal, even if you would 
rather some of them be temporary. 
(See pages 34-35  for a full list of 
milestone requirements and benefits.)
FOR EXAMPLE:
Golden Duck played a Trainer this round and Trained one of their employees. No other 
player has done this in this game so far, so Golden Duck places a checkmark on the 
“First to Train an Employee” milestone to show they have a $15 salary discount for 
the rest of the game. At the end of the round, every other player places an 
 on their 
boards on “First to Train an Employee” to show that they cannot gain this milestone 
anymore during this game.

Viewing Opponents’ Employees
Employees owned are always public information except while in hand and playing them to Work slots or On the beach in 
face-down stacks. Once stacks are revealed, all Employees are viewable again by anyone.
Mandatory Employee Actions
Most employee actions you use throughout the round are optional, but the following actions are considered mandatory. 
This means that if you play an employee with this action in your company structure, you are committed to using that action 
later in the round, even if it is no longer helpful to you.
•	Placing “Drive-In” signs (Local Manager, Regional Manager, pg. 17)
•	Lowering or raising your item price (Pricing Manager, Luxuries Manager, Discount Manager, pg. 27)
•	Earning cash bonuses (Waitress, CFO, pg. 27)
•	Receiving salary discounts (Recruiting Manager, HR Director, pg. 29)
As a general rule, actions you use in the Dinnertime and Payday phases are mandatory, but actions 
you use in the Working 9-5 phase are optional (with the exception of placing drive-in signs).
Restructuring Milestones
When you reveal your company structure, immediately claim any available 
milestones for “Working employees”, such as “First Waitress Played”. 
Employees On the beach are not working nor considered played for Milestones 
NOTE: The benefits of a “First Played” milestone will often apply to the employee you 
used to claim the milestone later on during the current round (pg. 34).
Gaining and Losing CEO Slots
When the bank breaks, reserve cards are revealed, then each player’s CEO may

Busy Marketeers, Items, and Duration
Once a Marketeer launches a campaign, they must 
remain busy running the campaign until it ends 
(i.e., you cannot end it early). While a Marketeer is 
busy, they are neither considered ‘played’ in a Work 
slot nor “On the beach”. Once a campaign is running, 
you cannot change its duration or prematurely cancel 
it. A campaign must continue running until its duration 
counters run out, even if it is no longer helpful to the 
player who launched it.
You cannot play a busy Marketeer, use their action, or Train them. 
You must pay their salary, and you cannot fire them (unless you are broke), pg. 29.
CAMPAIGN REACH
BILLBOARD AND MAILBOX 
CAMPAIGN REACH: The size of 
the token determines the area it 
reaches (except for mailboxes). 
Billboards reach all houses adjacent 
to them (a larger billboard can create 
demand on more houses (pg. 30).
An airplane with a 5-row flyover 
zone will generally reach more 
houses than an airplane with a 1-row

The reach of a mailbox campaign 
is not affected by its size, but a

All radio campaigns are the same size.

/CAMPAIGN NUMBER: Campaigns 
add demand to houses in order from 
lowest to highest number during 
the Marketing phase (pg. 30). 
STRATEGY TIP: Generally, a 
campaign that runs earlier is more likely 
to successfully place demand than

once a house reaches the limit of 3 
demand counters (or 5 with a garden),

Eternal Campaigns
If you have the “First Billboard Placed” milestone, all new 
marketing campaigns you run must be eternal, meaning they have

removed during the Marketing phase (pg. 30). A Marketeer running an eternal

PHASE 4. DINNERTIME
  
In the evening, it’s time for the city’s 
inhabitants to go out for dinner—and 
for you to reap the rewards of all the 
hard work you and your employees 
(but mostly you) have done. 
Players make no decisions during this 
phase. Instead, the customers living in 
each house decide whether to go out 
for dinner. If a house decides to eat at 
a player’s restaurant, that player must 
serve the house dinner, by selling the 
items the house wants from their stock 
and collecting cash for each item sold.
All houses are checked to determine 
if they eat or stay at home - one at 
a time, in numerical order - starting 
with the lowest-numbered house.
A house with no demand counters 
always stays at home.
If a house has any demand counters, 
check if there are any open restaurants 
it can eat at. In order for a house to 
eat at an open restaurant, it must 
meet both of these requirements:
•	 The restaurant must be connected by 
road to the house (at any distance); and
•	 The restaurant chain must have a full 
set of food and drink items in stock 
that exactly matches (or exceeds) 
the demand counters on the house.
If the house is not connected to any open restaurants, or if none of the open restaurants 
it is connected to are able to completely fulfill its demand, it decides to stay home. 
NOTE: To win a house’s business, a restaurant must be able to fulfill every demand 
counter on it. All demand counters remain on the house for this turn. If a player is short 
by just 1 item, the house will not eat at their restaurant. Customers are so picky!
If exactly one open restaurant fulfills both requirements, the house decides to eat at 
that restaurant (regardless of how far away it is or what prices it charges). If multiple 
open restaurants fulfill both requirements, competition ensues (next section).
FOR EXAMPLE: 
House 7 is checked first. 2 Restaurants have pizza, but none of them are connected to house 
7, so the house does not go out to eat, and the pizza demand counter on it stays. House 10 is 
checked next, and they want a burger and pizza, but no restaurant provides both,  so they do 
not eat, and the counters remain. Note that pizza is not sold to house 10 by itself. House 13 
wants pizza, and both restaurants have that, and they are both connected to the house! So now 
competition ensues to see which restaurant will actually sell that pizza - see the next section.
Competition
Whenever 2 or more restaurants compete for a house’s business, the house makes a 
final decision based on each restaurant’s item price and distance. Follow these steps:
A.	 ITEM PRICE: Determine the item price for each competing restaurant 
(see “Item Price” on the facing page for full details). 
B.	 DISTANCE: Next, determine the distance between the house and each 
competing restaurant. For each restaurant, start at the entrance and trace the 
least-distance path (i.e., fewest tile border crossings) by road to the house, then 
count how many times this path crosses over onto a new tile (pg. 10).
C.	 DECISION: Determine each competing restaurant’s effective price by 
adding together its item price and distance (each distance is considered as 
$1). The customers choose the restaurant with the lowest effective price. 
NOTE: This represents that customers are willing to walk 1 tile further to save $1 per 
item, 2 tiles to save $2, etc.
D.	 TIES: If there is a tie for the lowest effective price, the customers choose the 
restaurant owned by the player with more Waitresses played this turn (ignore 
those On the beach). If there is also a tie for most Waitresses, the customers 
choose the restaurant owned by the tied player who is earliest in turn order.

tiebreaker is initiated: Donkey played 
more Waitresses this turn, so house 13 
will eat the pizza at Donkey! See how 
much money they end up making in the

Stock
All food and drink items you gain are kept 
in your stock, which is a shared supply 
for all of your restaurants. Your stock is 
always public information. 
Demand
The demand counters on a house show 
which items the customers who live there 
want for dinner. Each house is limited to 
3 total demand counters at a time. If a 
house has a garden, this limit is increased 
to 5 demand counters. Houses never start 
with any demand counters; players must 
place demand counters on houses by 
running marketing campaigns (pg. 30).
If no houses have any demand counters, 
the entire Dinnertime phase can be 
skipped (except for Waitress and CFO cash 
bonuses; see below).
Dinnertime will always be skipped during 
the first 2 rounds of the game, because 
the earliest any house can receive 
demand counters is after Dinnertime 
in the second round. This preparatory 
stage can last for additional rounds if no 
one plays any early Marketeers.
Serving 
Customers and 
Getting Paid
If a house eats at your restaurant, you 
must immediately discard (to the supply) 
all demand counters on the house and 
an exact matching set of item counters 
from your stock. For each item sold, 
collect cash from the bank equal to your 
item price (including modifiers) plus any 
applicable cash bonuses (gardens, “First 
Marketed” Milestones, pg. 35).
Item Price 
1
5
Your item price is how much you are paid for each item you sell. You have one item 
price - all of your items are always the same price. For example, if your item price is 
$10, selling 1 burger and 1 beer gets you $20, regardless of which restaurant they 
were bought from. Ignore all calculations made regarding effective price (you are not 
paid more or less based on distance).
To calculate your item price, start with a base price of $10 and apply the following 
item price modifiers:
-$1 per Pricing Manager you played (ignore those On the beach).
-$3 per Discount Manager you played (ignore those On the beach).
+$10 if you have a Luxuries Manager played (ignore those On the beach). 
-$1 if you have the “First to Lower Prices” milestone (once gained, this is permanent 
and must always apply, (pg. 35).
All item price modifiers are mandatory, meaning you must apply each modifier showing 
on all of your played employees. Employees On the beach do not apply a modifier.
NOTE: Item price is not affected by the number of items sold, nor any cash bonuses that 
are earned from milestones or other sources (next section). 
Cash Bonuses
Cash bonuses provided by employees and milestones increase how much money 
you take home at Dinnertime. They are not included when calculating your effective 
price, so they have no effect on whether customers decide to eat at your restaurant.
HOUSES WITH GARDENS: After calculating your item price as detailed above, 
double the total if the house making the purchase has a garden!
“FIRST MARKETED” MILESTONES: When you sell items, if you have a “First 
Burger/Pizza/Drink Marketed” milestone, take $5 from the bank for each 
item of the corresponding type you sell (pg. 35). In the case of the “First Drink 
Marketed” milestone, this applies to every drink you sell (of all types). A garden 
does not double this bonus.
This is the total cash gained from this sale to this single house. 
Repeat the process above if you make another sale to another house. 
CFO NOTE: If you have a CFO, keep all money collected in a separate pile 
to keep track of how much you’ve gained this entire Dinnertime phase 
(see CFO Bonus below).
End of Dinnertime Cash Bonuses
There are two more possible bonuses, Waitresses and CFO; resolve Waitress first.
WAITRESSES: At the end of 
Dinnertime, take $3 from the bank for 
each Waitress played in your company 
structure (or $5 if you have the “First 
Waitress Played” milestone, (pg. 34). 
You earn this bonus even if you did not 
sell any items at Dinnertime.
CFO NOTE: If you have a CFO, add this 
Waitress money to the money you’ve 
already collected this Dinnertime phase 
(if any).
At the final cash bonus, process the CFO 
bonus if you have it!
CFO: At the end of Dinnertime, if you 
have a CFO in your company structure, 
collect a bonus equal to 50% of the total 
cash you earned this Dinnertime phase 
(rounded up). This includes cash earned 
from Waitresses and other cash bonuses.

Dinnertime Milestones
At the start of Dinnertime, if there is a Pricing or Discount Manager in your company structure, and the 
“First to Lower Prices” milestone is still available, immediately claim it and receive its benefit. Because all pricing 
employee actions are mandatory, you must claim this milestone even if you do not sell any items this phase.
At any point during Dinnertime, if you have enough total cash to claim an available “First to Have $X” 
milestone, immediately claim it. 
NOTE: Regardless of when you claim “First to Have $100”, your CEO may not use their 
new CFO ability until the following Dinnertime phase, (pg. 34).
FOR EXAMPLE: 
Donkey sold a pizza to house 13, so the 
demand counter is removed from house 
13, and 1 pizza counter is discarded 
from Donkey’s supply. The base price 
of all items is $10, and they played 
a Pricing Manager to reduce that by 
$1, resulting in an item price of $9. 
Notice how this is not the same as the 
effective price as seen in the previous 
example!
There is a garden attached to house 13, 
so this $9 is doubled to $18. Donkey has 
the “First Pizza Marketed” milestone, 
so they gain a $5 bonus for each pizza 
they sell. Total so far, Donkey is gaining 
a total of $23 for this single-pizza sale.
At the end of the round, $6 is gained 
from 2 played Waitresses, to make 
a total of $29. Then their CFO adds 
50% to that, to make their total 
cash gained this round $44. 
Notice how the garden does not 
impact the cash gained from the 
milestone or Waitresses.
Breaking the Bank
At any point during the Dinnertime phase, if the bank has $0 remaining, the bank 
breaks. The first time the bank breaks, immediately take the following steps:
1.	REVEAL RESERVE: Reveal the bank reserve cards that players selected at setup. 
2.	REFILL BANK: Sum together the cash amounts shown on the revealed cards. 
Refill the bank with that much cash from the game box. 
3.	 CEO SLOTS: Determine which number of CEO slots appears most often on the 
revealed cards. This is the number of slots each CEO has for the rest of the game. 
If two types of reserve cards occur equally often, the highest number determines 
the slots. For example, two “2 slots” cards and two “4 slots” cards will lead to all 
CEOs having 4 slots. If the number is 2 or 4, each player tucks their bank reserve 
card with the matching number of slots under their CEO to indicate their new 
number of slots (pg.13). Take the matching card out of the box if needed.
NOTE: If the number is 3, CEOs continue to have 3 slots, so players don’t need to tuck cards.
4.	FINISH PAYING: If the bank broke while a player was still owed cash, they 
continue taking what they were owed. Then continue the Dinnertime phase.
BREAKING THE BANK AGAIN
The second time the bank breaks, 
do not refill the bank. For the rest 
of the Dinnertime phase, players 
continue to earn cash normally, 
but they must either take the cash 
they earn from the game box or 
write it as an ‘IOU’ on a sheet of 
paper. At the end of Dinnertime, 
the game ends, and whoever has 
the most cash wins (pg. 33).

PHASE 6. MARKETING
Marketing campaigns create demand with no player decisions.
Campaigns Run
Campaigns run one at a time, in numerical order (start with the
lowest-numbered campaign). When each campaign runs, do the following :
A.	 Place 1 demand counter (taken 
from the supply) - of type matching 
the duration counter - on each 
house in that Campaign’s Reach. 
But note the following limitation:
•	A house can only have a 
maximum of 3 demand counters 
on it; or if it has a garden, the 
maximum is 5 instead. 
•	While a house is at its demand 
counter maximum, marketing 
campaigns have no effect on it. 
You cannot swap out an existing 
counter with a new one!  
THEMATIC NOTE: This can lead 
to some campaigns being utterly 
useless, as is the nature of some 
marketing campaigns!
B.	 Return 1 of the campaign’s  
duration counters to the supply 
(even if the campaign did not 
place any demand counters).
C.	 END OF CAMPAIGN CHECK: 
If the last duration counter was 
removed from the campaign, the 
campaign immediately ends. 
•	This is the only way a 
campaign can end!
•	When a campaign ends, return 
the token to the supply, the 
campaign’s Marketeer card 
goes On the beach, and the busy 
marker is returned to the supply.
Campaign Reach
Campaigns Reach depends on the 
type of Campaign as follows: 
Gardens are considered part of 
their house - treat the garden as an 
extension of the house, not as 
a separate part 
(i.e., a house 
with a garden 
is treated 
as a single 
6-square 
sized house).

A billboard campaign reaches each

PHASE 7. CLEANUP
After the Marketing phase, follow these steps to end the round:
A.	 THROW AWAY ITEMS: All players must return any unsold items in their stock to 
the supply. (If a player has the “First to Throw Away Food or Drink” milestone, they 
can keep up to 10 items in their freezer instead of throwing them away, (pg. 34.)
B.	 RETURN EMPLOYEES: Each player returns all played employees in their 
company structure and On the beach to their hand. Busy Marketeers are left faceup. 
NOTE: This can be a good time to ask to see an opponent’s cards, (pg. 13.
C.	 REMOVE SIGNS: Remove all “Coming Soon” and “Drive-In” signs on 
restaurants. All restaurants on the map are now open and all restaurants no 
longer have drive-ins.
D.	 CROSS OUT MILESTONES: Each player must place an 
 token on each milestone they no longer are able to claim (pg. 11).
E.	 NEW ROUND: Begin the next round with the Restructuring phase. All 
duration counters on marketing campaigns and all demand counters on 
houses remain in play.
Cleanup Milestones
If you throw away at least 1 food or drink item during the Cleanup phase while the “First 
to Throw Away Food or Drink” milestone is available, immediately claim the milestone. 
Although you gain a new freezer immediately, you may not use it to store unsold items 
until the following cleanup phase, so you must still throw away all unsold items you 
currently have (pg. 34).
Game End
After the bank breaks for the second 
time (pg. 28), finish the Dinnertime 
phase, then end the game. Do not resolve 
the remaining phases of the round. 
NOTE: You do not need to pay salaries 
in the last round—no need to fire your 
whole staff!
Each player counts up their total cash 
(including any “IOUs” they have written 
down). Whoever has the most cash 
wins. In the event of a tie, the tied player 
who is ahead in turn order wins.

 Some strategic pointers 
 
The best strategy in the game 
is highly dependent on the map 
layout, your map position, and the 
actions of other players 
(in no particular order).





Milestones are the key to planning 
your strategy. You will need to 
claim milestones before others do. 
Reaching the right milestones as 
soon as possible is often essential, 
as the others will likely be taken by 
the time you reach them.





There is a trade-off between 
building up your company through 
Hiring and Training, and earning 
cash through marketing and sales. 
The winning mix depends on how 
long the game ends up taking. 
This in turn depends on the bank 
reserve cards players chose, and 
whether more players choose 
to focus on recruiting and 
training for the long game or 
making early sales to break 
the bank quickly.



 

The game often accelerates 
dramatically towards the end. You 
can win by earning cash only in the 
last round, if you have prepared 
well—or you could still be preparing 
for a round that never comes.
